summary:
The Shaft Graves of [[event:57|Mycenae]] are a series of elite burial complexes at the citadel of Mycenae in the northeastern Peloponnese of Greece, dating to approximately 1600–1500 BC. They represent the earliest known elite burials of the Mycenaean civilization and were discovered by Heinrich Schliemann in 1876 in what he famously (though incorrectly) identified as the graves of Agamemnon and other heroes of the Trojan War. The graves are organized into two circles: Grave Circle A, located inside the later Lion Gate, and Grave Circle B outside the citadel walls.

The shaft graves contained extraordinarily rich assemblages of grave goods that document the sudden emergence of a powerful warrior elite in mainland Greece during the sixteenth century BC. Objects included gold death masks, golden cups and vessels, bronze swords and daggers inlaid with gold and silver hunting scenes, amber jewelry, ostrich eggs, and objects imported from Egypt, the Near East, and the Aegean. The famous gold funeral mask from Shaft Grave V, called the Mask of Agamemnon, is one of the most iconic artifacts from the ancient world.

The wealth represented in the shaft graves points to an elite that derived power from control of long-distance trade and warfare, and the assemblages show that Mycenae was already integrated into the broader Late Bronze Age palace economy of the eastern Mediterranean. The shaft graves mark the beginning of a cultural tradition that would culminate in the palace-based Mycenaean civilization of the fourteenth and thirteenth centuries BC, before its collapse during the [[event:1815|Bronze Age Collapse]].
